name: Krishna Yadav

projects.title: Sales Dashboard in Power BI
projects.problem: Sales leaders needed a deployed dashboard experience to monitor revenue, profit, orders, regional performance, and KPI movement quickly.
projects.role: Designed the dashboard story, organized KPI sections, built the deployed analytics interface, and shaped the recruiter-facing live view.
projects.approach: Created a live sales dashboard with KPI cards, performance visuals, regional insights, and a clean layout for fast business review.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live dashboard experience that makes sales trends, weak regions, and target performance easier to scan.
projects.impact: Tracked 8 sales KPIs across region, category, revenue, profit, orders, growth, returns, and target achievement.

projects.title: HR CV Shortlister
projects.problem: Recruiters need a faster deployed tool to review resumes, compare candidate profiles, and shortlist relevant applicants.
projects.role: Built the shortlisting flow, structured candidate evaluation, and deployed the project for HR screening use cases.
projects.approach: Used automation-focused logic to collect candidate inputs, compare role fit, and support faster screening decisions.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live HR tech tool that reduces manual review effort and improves candidate filtering workflow.
projects.impact: Designed to reduce resume screening time by up to 40% by prioritizing role-fit signals before manual review.

projects.title: Real-Time Sales Analytics (PULSE)
projects.problem: Sales teams needed a live, streaming view of transactions instead of stale end-of-day reports to react to demand shifts as they happen.
projects.role: Designed the streaming pipeline architecture and built the deployed real-time dashboard interface.
projects.approach: Built a Kafka-based ingestion pipeline where POS and web events flow through a Python consumer for validation and enrichment, land in Postgres, and surface live in a Power BI-style DirectQuery dashboard.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live-updating dashboard showing revenue, orders, average order value, and conversion rate with a real-time transaction feed.
projects.impact: Modeled a full Kafka to Python to SQL to BI pipeline capable of processing streaming sales events with sub-15-second dashboard refresh.

projects.title: Financial Performance Dashboard (Ledger)
projects.problem: Finance teams needed a consolidated view of revenue, cost, and margin trends without manually reconciling spreadsheets each period.
projects.role: Built the dashboard's data model, KPI logic, and the deployed analytics interface, including a data upload flow for custom datasets.
projects.approach: Structured a SQL-based fact and dimension model feeding DAX measures for Net Margin % and YoY Growth, with support for connecting a CSV, Excel, Power BI export, or live API to recompute the dashboard.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live financial dashboard covering revenue vs. expenses vs. profit trends, expense mix, and a period-by-period ledger detail view.
projects.impact: Built reusable SQL and DAX logic (Net Margin %, YoY Growth) that recomputes automatically for any connected dataset matching the schema.

projects.title: Churn Radar Dashboard
projects.problem: Businesses struggle to spot at-risk customers early, leading to lost revenue from preventable churn.
projects.role: Designed the churn analysis workflow, built the deployed dashboard interface, and structured the risk-scoring visuals.
projects.approach: Built a live churn analytics dashboard that segments customers by risk level, surfaces key churn drivers, and visualizes retention trends for fast decision-making.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live dashboard that helps identify high-risk customer segments and prioritize retention efforts.
projects.impact: Enabled quick identification of at-risk customer segments through visual risk scoring and trend tracking.

projects.title: E-Commerce Sales Analytics
projects.problem: E-commerce leadership needed a single view across platforms, categories, and customer segments to spot where revenue growth wasn't reaching profit.
projects.role: Designed the multi-tab analytics structure and built the deployed dashboard covering overview, customers, products, platforms, and profitability.
projects.approach: Analyzed a synthetic dataset of 6,000 customers, 320 products, and 34,185 orders across platforms to build revenue, margin, LTV/CAC, delivery, and return-rate visuals.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live command-center dashboard revealing that marketplace commissions change profitability rankings even when a platform leads on raw revenue.
projects.impact: Surfaced actionable signals such as category-level return-rate drivers and weak-ROI acquisition channels across 34,185 orders.

projects.title: Corex Enterprises
projects.problem: Corex Enterprises needed a deployed business website with a modern first impression, service clarity, and responsive presentation.
projects.role: Created the frontend structure, organized business content, improved page sections, and prepared the site for Netlify deployment.
projects.approach: Built a clean React-based website flow with reusable sections, professional visual hierarchy, and mobile-friendly styling.
projects.outcome: Delivered a live business website that supports service discovery, brand trust, and future lead capture improvements.
projects.impact: Created a deployed company website with responsive sections, clear service discovery, and production-ready presentation.

projects.title: Self-Learning AI for Games
projects.problem: Demonstrate reinforcement-style learning by training an AI agent to play Flappy Bird without hard-coded gameplay rules.
projects.role: Built the Python simulation, integrated NEAT training, and visualized agent performance across generations.
projects.approach: Integrated NEAT-Python with Pygame and visualized training behavior using Python analysis libraries.
projects.outcome: Created an adaptive AI simulation that learns through neuroevolution and performance feedback.
projects.impact: Improved agent survival across training generations and visualized model learning behavior for clear evaluation.

projects.title: Quantum Error Correction Simulation
projects.problem: Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum systems require benchmarking of fidelity, error rates, and mitigation strategies.
projects.role: Implemented simulation workflows and compared QEC behavior across fidelity and error-rate benchmarks.
projects.approach: Simulated QEC protocols with IBM Qiskit and Google Cirq, comparing resource-efficient designs for chemistry and optimization use cases.
projects.outcome: Built benchmark logic around qubit overhead, decoherence, and hybrid QEC-mitigation strategies.
projects.impact: Compared fidelity and error-rate behavior across simulation runs to support more reliable experiment decisions.

certifications: Python for Data Science - IBM PY0101EN

blogPosts.title: How to Build an Excel Dashboard From Scratch (Step-by-Step Guide)
blogPosts.summary: A practical walkthrough for turning raw spreadsheet data into a clean, interactive Excel dashboard with KPIs, charts, and slicers.

content.text: An Excel dashboard is one of the fastest ways to turn a messy spreadsheet into something a manager can actually use to make decisions. This guide walks through the exact process I follow when building one, from raw data to a finished, interactive dashboard.
Step 1: Get the Data Into a Proper Table

Before any chart gets built, the raw data needs to sit inside an Excel Table (Insert > Table), not just a plain range. Tables auto-expand as new rows are added, keep formulas consistent, and make every downstream step easier, especially PivotTables and slicers.
Step 2: Clean the Data With Power Query

Use Power Query (Data > Get & Transform) to remove duplicates, fix inconsistent date formats, trim whitespace, and split combined columns. Doing this once in Power Query means the cleanup step reruns automatically every time the source data refreshes, instead of manual cleanup every month.
Step 3: Decide the KPIs Before Building Any Chart

The biggest mistake in dashboard building is starting with charts instead of questions. Pick 4-8 KPIs that actually matter to the business, revenue, profit margin, order volume, top region, or customer churn are common examples, and design every visual around answering those questions clearly.
Step 4: Build the Core Visuals

items: PivotTables and PivotCharts for summarizing large datasets quickly
Line charts for trends over time (monthly revenue, order growth)
Bar charts for comparisons across categories (region, product, team)
Card-style cells (large bold numbers) for single headline KPIs
Conditional formatting or heatmaps to highlight outliers at a glance

content.text: Step 5: Add Interactivity With Slicers and Dropdowns

Slicers connected to PivotTables let anyone viewing the dashboard filter by region, date, or category without touching a formula. Data validation dropdowns paired with INDEX/MATCH or XLOOKUP can drive dynamic summary views for non-technical users.
Step 6: Design for Clarity, Not Decoration

Keep the color palette to two or three consistent colors, align everything to a grid, and remove gridlines and unnecessary borders. A dashboard should be scannable in under ten seconds, if a viewer has to hunt for the number that matters, the layout needs simplifying.
Common Mistakes to Avoid

items: Hardcoding values instead of linking to the data table
Too many colors or 3D chart effects that reduce readability
Mixing raw data sheets with the dashboard view instead of separating them
Skipping data validation, so a single bad row breaks every chart

content.text: Once the structure is solid, this same workflow, clean data, clear KPIs, purposeful charts, translates directly into tools like Power BI, which is usually the next step after Excel dashboards.

blogPosts.title: Power BI for Beginners: A Practical Starting Guide
blogPosts.summary: Everything a first-time Power BI user needs to go from installing the tool to publishing an interactive report: data loading, modeling, DAX basics, and more.

content.text: Power BI is Microsoft's business intelligence tool for turning raw data into interactive reports and dashboards. This guide covers the core workflow every beginner needs to understand before building their first real report.
1. Install Power BI Desktop

Power BI Desktop is free and available directly from Microsoft. It is where reports get built; the separate Power BI Service (app.powerbi.com) is where finished reports get published and shared with a team.
2. Connect to a Data Source
Use Get Data to connect to Excel files, CSVs, SQL Server, or web APIs. For a first project, an Excel sales dataset is the easiest starting point since the structure is already familiar.
3. Clean Data in Power Query Editor

Every connected table opens in Power Query Editor first. This is where column types get fixed, blank rows get removed, and columns get renamed before anything touches a chart. Treat this step as non-negotiable, dashboards built on unclean data are unreliable no matter how good the visuals look.
4. Build the Data Model (Relationships)

Real projects usually involve multiple tables, sales, products, regions, dates. In the Model view, connect these tables using relationships (usually one-to-many) so a single slicer can filter every visual across the report. A clean star schema, one central fact table connected to smaller dimension tables, keeps performance fast and DAX formulas simple.
5. Learn Basic DAX
DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) is the formula language behind every calculated metric. Three functions cover most beginner needs:

items: SUM() and AVERAGE() for basic aggregations
CALCULATE() to apply filters to an aggregation, the single most important DAX function to learn early
SUMX() for row-by-row calculations before summing, useful for metrics like revenue = price × quantity

content.text: 6. Build the First Visuals

Start simple: a bar chart for category comparison, a line chart for trend over time, and a few KPI cards for headline numbers. Add slicers for date range and category so the report becomes interactive rather than a static image.
7. Publish and Share
Once the report looks right, use Publish to push it to the Power BI Service, where it can be shared as a link, embedded, or scheduled to refresh automatically from the original data source.
Where to Go Next

After the basics, the next skills worth learning are DAX time-intelligence functions (like SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR), bookmarks for interactive navigation, and Power BI Service workspaces for team collaboration.

blogPosts.title: 20 SQL Interview Questions Every Data Analyst Should Know
blogPosts.summary: A concise set of SQL interview questions and answers covering joins, aggregations, subqueries, and window functions, organized from basic to advanced.

content.text: These are the SQL questions that come up most often in data analyst interviews, grouped by difficulty. Understanding the reasoning behind each answer matters more than memorizing the syntax.
Basics

items: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING? WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after a GROUP BY / aggregation has been applied.

What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP? DELETE removes rows and can be rolled back; TRUNCATE removes all rows quickly and resets identity but can't be filtered; DROP removes the entire table structure.
What is a primary key vs a foreign key? A primary key uniquely identifies each row in a table; a foreign key references a primary key in another table to enforce a relationship.
What does DISTINCT do? It removes duplicate rows from the result set.

What is the difference between UNION and UNION ALL? UNION removes duplicate rows across the combined result sets; UNION ALL keeps every row, including duplicates, and is faster since it skips the duplicate check.

content.text: Joins and Grouping

items: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and LEFT JOIN? INNER JOIN returns only matching rows from both tables; LEFT JOIN returns all rows from the left table plus matches from the right, filling unmatched columns with NULL.
What does a self join do? It joins a table to itself, typically used for hierarchical data like an employee table referencing a manager's ID in the same table.
How do you find duplicate rows in a table? Group by the columns that define a duplicate and use HAVING COUNT(*) > 1.
What is the difference between GROUP BY and ORDER BY? GROUP BY aggregates rows into summary groups; ORDER BY simply sorts the final result set.
What happens if you SELECT a column that isn't in the GROUP BY clause or an aggregate function? Most databases will throw an error, since it's ambiguous which row's value should represent the group.

content.text: Subqueries and Intermediate Concepts

items: What is a subquery? A query nested inside another query, often used in WHERE or FROM clauses to filter or compute intermediate results.

What is the difference between a subquery and a JOIN? A JOIN combines columns from multiple tables into one result set; a subquery typically returns a single value or list used to filter another query. JOINs are often faster for large datasets.
What is a Common Table Expression (CTE)? A named temporary result set defined with WITH, used to make complex queries more readable by breaking them into logical steps.

What is the difference between a correlated and a non-correlated subquery? A correlated subquery references a column from the outer query and runs once per outer row; a non-correlated subquery runs independently and only once.

content.text: Advanced: Window Functions and Performance

items: What is a window function? A calculation across a set of related rows (a 'window') without collapsing them into a single output row, unlike GROUP BY. Common examples: ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), and SUM() OVER().
What is the difference between RANK() and DENSE_RANK()? RANK() skips numbers after a tie (1,2,2,4); DENSE_RANK() does not (1,2,2,3).
How would you find the second-highest salary in a table? Use LIMIT/OFFSET, a subquery with MAX() excluding the top value, or DENSE_RANK() filtered to rank 2.
What is an index and why does it matter? An index is a data structure that speeds up row lookups on specific columns, at the cost of slightly slower writes and extra storage.
What is normalization? The process of structuring tables to reduce data redundancy, typically by splitting data into related tables connected by keys.

content.text: In an interview, walking through your reasoning out loud, why a LEFT JOIN instead of INNER, why a CTE instead of a nested subquery, matters more than getting the syntax perfect on the first try.

blogPosts.title: Data Analyst Roadmap: Skills, Tools, and Learning Path
blogPosts.summary: A structured path from zero to job-ready data analyst: which skills to learn first, which tools matter, and how to build a portfolio that gets interviews.

content.text: There are a lot of overlapping tools in the data analytics space, and it's easy to spend months learning things in the wrong order. This is the sequence that gets someone from beginner to job-ready the fastest.
Step 1: Excel (2-3 weeks)

Excel is still the most commonly used analytics tool in real companies. Focus on PivotTables, VLOOKUP/XLOOKUP, basic charting, and simple dashboards before moving on. This step also builds the spreadsheet-thinking mindset that makes SQL and Power BI easier to pick up later.
Step 2: SQL (3-4 weeks)

SQL is non-negotiable for almost every data analyst role. Learn SELECT/WHERE/GROUP BY first, then JOINs, then subqueries and CTEs, then window functions. Practicing on a real dataset (even a free one from Kaggle) matters more than reading theory.
Step 3: Data Visualization - Power BI or Tableau (4-6 weeks)

Pick one BI tool and go deep rather than learning both shallowly. Power BI is the more in-demand choice in most markets right now. Learn data modeling, DAX basics, and how to design a dashboard around business KPIs, not just how to make charts.
Step 4: Python or R for Analysis (4-6 weeks)

Python with Pandas and NumPy is the more common choice for analyst roles. Focus on data cleaning, exploratory data analysis, and basic visualization with Matplotlib or Seaborn. Machine learning is optional at this stage, most analyst roles need clean analysis, not model building.
Step 5: Statistics Fundamentals (2-3 weeks)

Mean, median, standard deviation, correlation vs causation, and basic hypothesis testing come up constantly in interviews and real reporting work. This doesn't need to be a full statistics course, just enough to reason correctly about data.
Step 6: Build 3-4 Real Portfolio Projects

Recruiters want to see a project that tells a business story: a problem, the approach taken, and a measurable outcome, not just a chart with no context. A sales dashboard, an HR or churn analysis tool, and one end-to-end SQL-to-BI pipeline project cover most of what recruiters look for.
Step 7: Apply, Get Feedback, Iterate

Start applying before every skill feels 'complete'. Real interview feedback, the questions that stump you, the gaps that show up, is a faster teacher than more tutorials. Keep refining the portfolio and resume based on what actually comes up in interviews.
Realistic Timeline

For someone studying consistently, 4-6 months covers the full path from beginner to job-ready, faster with prior technical background, slower if learning part-time alongside other commitments. Consistency matters more than speed.
